narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong commitment to pursuing a legal career, along with your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 32, lower-middle family income background, and your plan to attend full-time. The admissions model evaluated your profile to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated an 88.3% probability that you would fail— a risk level that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed on that critical test. This prediction wasn't based on your race, gender, or family income alone, but primarily on a few key academic indicators from your record that stood out as concerns. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is in the bottom tenth compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position around the 5th or 6th decile— this suggested to the model a potential late-stage struggle in maintaining competitive grades. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile also at 1, well below the average of about the 6th decile, reinforcing a pattern of early underperformance relative to peers who tend to rank higher. Your LSAT score of 32 further nudged the prediction toward failure, as it's noticeably lower than the dataset average of 36.7 seen among students who typically pass the bar. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was close to the average of 3.21 and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these weren't strong enough to offset the top concerns. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your lower decile rankings in both early and later years deviated most sharply from their stronger academic trajectories. This combination painted a picture of higher risk in the model's eyes, even accounting for your personal background. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, while also reflecting on strategies to improve your class rankings in any future graduate work—perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience as a Hispanic male from a lower-middle income family could help in reapplying, maybe with additional letters of recommendation highlighting your potential. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so targeted improvements could position you much better for the next cycle. Keep pushing; your determination is clear, and with these adjustments, law school doors can reopen.
